attributes.stem:
A 23-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department at 24 weeks gestation with increasing intensity and frequency of headaches over the past two days. Her first pregnancy was complicated by preeclampsia requiring induction of labour at 35 weeks. This pregnancy was conceived with a new partner. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears uncomfortable. Her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 165/95 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following clinical findings would be most concerning for risk of eclampsia?
options: Elevated liver enzyme with ALP 200 U/L
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features concerning for preeclampsia in a high-risk pregnancy. Preeclampsia is diagnosed as hypertension after 20 weeks gestation with evidence of end-organ dysfunction.

Sustained ankle clonus is the most concerning finding as it indicates significant central nervous system irritability and is a strong predictor of impending eclampsia. These neurological signs suggest cerebral edema and increased intracranial pressure, which can rapidly progress to seizures, stroke, or death. When these signs are present, delivery planning is indicated regardless of gestational age to prevent maternal mortality.

Sustained blood pressure of 165/95 mmHg is concerning but not as immediately threatening as neurological signs. With appropriate antihypertensive therapy and close monitoring the hypertension may be managed and eclampsia risk reduced.

Elevated liver enzyme with ALP 200 U/L is less specific for preeclampsia severity. While liver involvement can occur in preeclampsia, ALP is often elevated in normal pregnancy due to placental production. Transaminases (ALT/AST) are more specific indicators of liver damage in preeclampsia and HELLP syndrome. This level of ALP alone would not necessarily indicate severe preeclampsia.

Urine protein creatinine ratio of 25 is a level within the normal range and not diagnostic of preeclampsia.

Lower limb oedema is a common finding in normal pregnancy and, while it can be more pronounced in preeclampsia, it is not a specific indicator of disease severity or imminent eclampsia. Many women experience dependent oedema during pregnancy without developing preeclampsia or eclampsia. More significant oedema is oedema of hands or face.

When hyperreflexia and clonus are present it is important to consider management with magnesium sulphate and plan delivery to reduce the chance of an eclamptic seizure.
learningPoints: Hyperreflexia and clonus are important neurological signs that predict impending eclampsia in preeclampsia.
Severe features of preeclampsia include blood pressure ≥160/110 mmHg, neurological symptoms, thrombocytopenia, and impaired liver or renal function.
Clinical signs of severe preeclampsia with neurologic irritability require management with magnesium sulphate to prevent eclampsia.
references: Lowe SA, Bowyer L, Lust K, McMahon LP, North RA, Paech M, et al. The SOMANZ guideline for the management of hypertensive disorders of pregnancy. In: Society for obstetric medicine in Australia and New Zealand [online]. 2014 [cited 2020 Dec 31]. Available at: https://www.somanz.org/.

Galaviz-Hernandez C, Sosa-Macias M, Teran E, Garcia-Ortiz JE, Lazalde-Ramos BP. Paternal Determinants in Preeclampsia. Front Physiol. 2019;9:1870. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30666213/

Altman D, Carroli G, Duley L, et al. Do women with pre-eclampsia, and their babies, benefit from magnesium sulphate? The Magpie Trial: a randomised placebo-controlled trial. Lancet. 2002;359(9321):1877-1890. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12057549/

Doyle LW, Crowther CA, Middleton P, Marret S, Rouse D. Magnesium sulphate for women at risk of preterm birth for neuroprotection of the fetus. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2009;(1):CD004661. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19160238/
Agius A, Sultana R, Camenzuli C, Calleja-Agius J, Balzan R. An update on the genetics of pre-eclampsia. Minerva Ginecol. 2018;70(4):465-479. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28994563/

Laskowska M, Bednarek A. Optimizing Delivery Strategies in Eclampsia: A Comprehensive Review on Seizure Management and Birth Methods. Med Sci Monit. 2023 Oct 7;29:e941709. doi: 10.12659/MSM.941709. PMID: 37803822; PMCID: PMC10566307.
